quiz_questions.question_text: A disk with I = 2 kg·m² rotates at 5 rad/s. What is its angular momentum?
quiz_questions.explanation: L = Iω = 2 × 5 = 10 kg·m²/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 0.3 kg ball moves at 4 m/s in a circle of radius 0.5 m. What is its angular momentum about the center?
If no external torque acts on a system, what is conserved?
quiz_questions.explanation: When the net external torque on a system is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L_initial = L_final.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spinning figure skater pulls her arms in, reducing her moment of inertia from 4 to 2 kg·m². If she was spinning at 3 rad/s, what is her new angular velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: L is conserved: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂. ω₂ = (4 × 3)/2 = 6 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of angular momentum?
quiz_questions.explanation: Angular momentum L = Iω has units of kg·m²/s (equivalent to N·m·s or J·s).

quiz_questions.question_text: A point particle of mass 2 kg moves at 3 m/s along a straight line that passes 4 m from a fixed point. What is its angular momentum about that point?
quiz_questions.explanation: L = mvr⊥ = 2 × 3 × 4 = 24 kg·m²/s. Here r⊥ is the perpendicular distance from the point to the line of motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: A torque of 6 N·m is applied to a wheel for 3 s. What is the change in angular momentum?
quiz_questions.explanation: ΔL = τ × Δt = 6 × 3 = 18 kg·m²/s. This is the rotational analog of impulse.

quiz_questions.question_text: A disk and a ring of the same mass and radius start with the same angular momentum. Which has the greater angular velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: I_disk = ½MR² < I_ring = MR². Since L = Iω, a smaller I requires a larger ω for the same L. The disk spins faster.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ice skater increases her angular velocity from 2 to 6 rad/s. By what factor did her moment of inertia change?
quiz_questions.explanation: Conservation of angular momentum: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂. I₂/I₁ = ω₁/ω₂ = 2/6 = 1/3. Her I decreased to one-third.

quiz_questions.question_text: The angular impulse–angular momentum theorem states:
quiz_questions.explanation: The angular impulse–momentum theorem: ΔL = τ_net × Δt. It is the rotational analog of the impulse–momentum theorem.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is angular momentum?

options.text: The product of force and angular displacement
The rate of change of angular velocity
The energy stored in a rotating object
The rotational analog of linear momentum; L = Iω for a rigid body

quiz_questions.explanation: Angular momentum (L) is the rotational analog of linear momentum. For a rigid body, L = Iω; for a particle, L = r × p.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the law of conservation of angular momentum?

options.text: Angular momentum equals the product of torque and time
If no net external torque acts on a system, its total angular momentum remains constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Conservation of angular momentum: when the net external torque on a system is zero, the total angular momentum is constant (L_i = L_f).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is angular impulse?

options.text: The change in angular momentum; equal to τ_net × Δt
The product of angular velocity and time
The torque at the start of rotation

quiz_questions.explanation: Angular impulse is τ_net × Δt. By the angular impulse–momentum theorem, it equals the change in angular momentum: ΔL = τ_net Δt.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for angular momentum to be a 'vector quantity'?

options.text: Its magnitude depends on the reference frame
It is the same as angular velocity
It has both magnitude and direction, with direction along the axis of rotation (by the right-hand rule)

quiz_questions.explanation: Angular momentum is a vector: L = Iω (for rigid body) or L = r × p (for a particle). Its direction is along the rotation axis, determined by the right-hand rule.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'precession' in the context of angular momentum?

options.text: The slowing down of a spinning object
The increase in angular velocity when torque is applied
The slow rotation of the angular momentum vector (and spin axis) around an external torque direction, as seen in a spinning top or gyroscope
The wobbling of an unbalanced rotor

quiz_questions.explanation: Precession is the gradual rotation of the spin axis of a rotating body when an external torque is applied perpendicular to the angular momentum. The rate is Ω = τ/L.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does τ = dL/dt represent?

options.text: The power delivered by a torque
Newton's second law in rotational form: net torque equals the time rate of change of angular momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: τ_net = dL/dt is the rotational form of Newton's second law. It generalizes τ = Iα to cases where I may change.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the angular momentum of a particle?

options.text: L = r × p = r × mv; a vector perpendicular to both position and momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: For a particle, L = r × p = r × (mv). Its magnitude is L = mvr sinθ, and its direction is perpendicular to both r and v.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'gyroscope'?

options.text: A wheel that always points north
A pendulum that swings in a circle
A spinning object whose angular momentum makes it resist changes in orientation
A device that measures angular acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: A gyroscope is a rapidly spinning object that resists changes to its orientation due to conservation of angular momentum. It is used in navigation and stabilization.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'angular momentum transfer' refer to?

options.text: Angular momentum disappearing from a system
The conversion of angular momentum to linear momentum
The process by which torque from one body changes the angular momentum of another

quiz_questions.explanation: Angular momentum transfer occurs when internal torques between objects redistribute angular momentum, while the total angular momentum of the system remains constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the relationship between angular momentum and rotational kinetic energy?

options.text: Both B and C are correct

quiz_questions.explanation: KE = ½Iω² = ½(Iω)ω = ½Lω. Also KE = L²/(2I). Both expressions are valid.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50 kg child runs at 4 m/s tangent to the rim of a stationary merry-go-round (I = 600 kg·m², radius 2 m) and jumps on. What is the final angular velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: L_child = mvr = 50 × 4 × 2 = 400 kg·m²/s. I_child on rim = mr² = 50(4) = 200 kg·m². I_total = 600 + 200 = 800 kg·m². ω_f = L/I_total = 400/800 = 0.5 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two identical disks (each I = 3 kg·m²) spin about the same vertical axis. Disk 1 spins at 10 rad/s and Disk 2 at 4 rad/s in the same direction. They are pressed together until they reach a common angular velocity. What is it?
quiz_questions.explanation: L_total = Iω₁ + Iω₂ = 3(10) + 3(4) = 42 kg·m²/s. I_total = 3 + 3 = 6 kg·m². ω_f = 42/6 = 7 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A neutron star collapses and its radius shrinks by a factor of 1000. If it originally rotated once every 10 days, what is the new rotation period?
quiz_questions.explanation: For a uniform sphere: I ∝ R². Conserving angular momentum: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂. Since ω = 2π/T: R₁²/T₁ = R₂²/T₂. T₂ = T₁ × (R₂/R₁)² = 10 days × (1/1000)² = 10 × 86400 s × 10⁻⁶ = 864000 × 10⁻⁶ = 0.864 s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50 kg diver leaves the board with angular momentum 20 kg·m²/s. In tuck position her I is 4 kg·m²; in pike position her I is 8 kg·m². What are her angular velocities in each position?
quiz_questions.explanation: Angular momentum is conserved during the dive (no external torques about her center of mass). Tuck: ω = L/I = 20/4 = 5 rad/s. Pike: ω = 20/8 = 2.5 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A turntable (I = 0.1 kg·m²) rotates freely at 10 rad/s. A 0.05 kg piece of clay is dropped vertically onto it at r = 0.2 m from the center. What is the new angular velocity?
A playground roundabout (I = 200 kg·m²) spins at 2 rad/s. A 40 kg child at the rim (r = 1.5 m) moves to r = 0.5 m. What is the new angular velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: I_child_initial = 40(1.5)² = 90 kg·m². I_total_i = 200 + 90 = 290 kg·m². I_child_final = 40(0.25) = 10 kg·m². I_total_f = 200 + 10 = 210 kg·m². ω_f = 290(2)/210 = 580/210 = 2.76 rad/s ≈ 2.70 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bullet (0.01 kg, 400 m/s) strikes and embeds in a thin rod (2 kg, 1 m long) at its end. The rod is pivoted at the other end. What is the angular velocity just after impact?
quiz_questions.explanation: L_bullet = mvr = 0.01 × 400 × 1 = 4 kg·m²/s. After impact: I_rod = (1/3)(2)(1) = 0.667 kg·m². I_bullet = 0.01(1)² = 0.01 kg·m². I_total = 0.677 kg·m². ω = L/I = 4/0.677 = 5.91 ≈ 6 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two skaters (each 60 kg) hold hands and spin around their midpoint at 3 rad/s, 0.8 m apart. They pull together to 0.4 m apart. What is the new angular velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: Initially each is 0.4 m from center. I_i = 2 × 60 × (0.4)² = 2(60)(0.16) = 19.2 kg·m². After: each is 0.2 m from center. I_f = 2 × 60 × (0.2)² = 2(60)(0.04) = 4.8 kg·m². ω_f = I_iω_i/I_f = 19.2(3)/4.8 = 57.6/4.8 = 12 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A disk (I = 0.5 kg·m²) spins at 12 rad/s clockwise. A second disk (I = 0.3 kg·m²) spins at 8 rad/s counterclockwise. They are coupled together. What is the final angular velocity and direction?
quiz_questions.explanation: Taking clockwise as positive: L = 0.5(12) + 0.3(−8) = 6 − 2.4 = 3.6 kg·m²/s. I_total = 0.8 kg·m². ω_f = 3.6/0.8 = 4.5 rad/s clockwise.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spacecraft (I = 1000 kg·m²) is initially not rotating. An internal reaction wheel (I = 5 kg·m²) spins up to 600 rad/s. What angular velocity does the spacecraft acquire?
quiz_questions.explanation: Total angular momentum must remain zero (no external torques). I_wheel ω_wheel + I_craft ω_craft = 0. ω_craft = −I_wheel ω_wheel / I_craft = −5(600)/1000 = −3 rad/s. The spacecraft rotates opposite to the wheel.
